reason: Both answers correctly hit the core judo (change the axis, weaponize complexity/breadth, target underserved segments, get ahead of the RFP). B edges ahead with two genuinely new, actionable ideas not present in A: pushing to change the bake-off format itself (hands-on pilot vs. RFP grid, with the honest caveat that if you can't win a pilot either it's a product problem), and the concrete diagnostic of asking your last three wins why they chose you. These aren't padding — they're specific tactics that would change what the asker does next. A is solid and equally concise but doesn't add anything B lacks.
